skill.name: statistical-analysis
skill.description: Guided statistical analysis for test selection, assumption checks, power analysis, and APA-style reporting. Use when you need to choose an appropriate statistical test for your data and produce publication-ready results (including effect sizes and diagnostics).
skill.summary: Guided statistical test selection, assumption diagnostics, power analysis, and APA-style reporting for research data.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 175 static analysis findings were evaluated as FALSE POSITIVES. The 115 'external_commands/Ruby shell backtick execution' findings are Markdown code fence blocks (```python) in documentation files, not shell execution. The 60+ 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings reference statistical functions like stats.shapiro() (Shapiro-Wilk normality test), not cryptography. The 'Dynamic import' finding is a standard multi-line Python import statement. The 'Crypto seed/private key mention' refers to a Bayes theorem mnemonic. The only TRUE POSITIVE findings are 3 hardcoded URLs pointing to legitimate GitHub and OSF repositories for academic reference. The skill is a legitimate statistical analysis reference guide with no malicious intent or behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Reference URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md and reporting_standards.md contain hardcoded URLs pointing to the source GitHub repository and an OSF data sharing template. These are legitimate educational references for the statistical analysis skill, not malicious endpoints. No credentials or sensitive data are transmitted.

content.user_title: Run Statistical Analysis with AI Guidance
content.value_statement: Researchers often struggle to select the correct statistical test and report results properly. This skill provides step-by-step guidance for test selection, assumption checking, effect size calculation, power analysis, and APA-style reporting.
actual_capabilities: Guides test selection based on research design, variable types, and data distribution properties
Runs assumption checks including normality tests, homogeneity of variance, and outlier detection
Calculates effect sizes such as Cohen's d, partial eta-squared, and Cramer's V with confidence intervals
Performs a priori and sensitivity power analysis using statsmodels
Generates APA-style reporting strings with test statistics, degrees of freedom, p-values, and effect sizes
Provides Bayesian analysis guidance with posterior summaries, credible intervals, and Bayes Factors
limitations: Does not execute Python code directly; relies on the AI to write and run code in the conversation
Cannot access or analyze user-provided datasets stored outside the conversation context
Bayesian workflows require pymc and arviz libraries that may not be available in all environments
APA-style templates follow general guidelines and may need adjustment for specific journal requirements

use_cases.title: Selecting the Correct Statistical Test
use_cases.description: A graduate student has collected survey data and needs to choose between a t-test, ANOVA, or non-parametric alternative. The skill guides them through research design questions and variable type analysis to select the appropriate test.

use_cases.title: Planning a Study with Power Analysis
use_cases.description: A research coordinator is designing a clinical trial and needs to determine the required sample size. The skill guides them through a priori power analysis to ensure the study has adequate statistical power before data collection begins.

use_cases.title: Reviewing Statistical Reporting Quality
use_cases.description: A journal reviewer needs to verify whether a manuscript follows APA reporting standards for statistical results. The skill provides checklists and templates to evaluate completeness of reported statistics.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Test Selection Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: I have two groups of participants (treatment and control) and a continuous outcome variable. The data appears roughly normal. What statistical test should I use, and what assumptions should I check?
prompt_templates.scenario: A user needs help choosing between a t-test, Mann-Whitney U, or ANOVA for their study design.

prompt_templates.title: Full Analysis with Assumption Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a dataset with three groups and a continuous outcome. Please help me check normality and variance assumptions, run the appropriate test with effect size, and provide an APA-style result string.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user wants a complete analysis pipeline from assumption verification through final reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Bayesian Analysis Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to run a Bayesian t-test as an alternative to the frequentist approach. Please guide me through prior selection, posterior interpretation, and reporting Bayes Factors with credible intervals.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user familiar with frequentist statistics wants to explore Bayesian alternatives for their analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Power Analysis for Grant Proposals
prompt_templates.prompt: I am writing a grant proposal and need to justify my sample size. I expect a medium effect size (d = 0.5) and want 90% power at alpha = 0.05. Please guide me through the power analysis and help me write the methods section.
prompt_templates.scenario: A researcher preparing a grant application needs to calculate and justify sample size requirements.

output_examples.input: Run a two-sample t-test comparing Group A and Group B with APA-style reporting.
output_examples.output: Group A (n = 50, M = 75.23, SD = 9.12) and Group B (n = 52, M = 69.87, SD = 8.95) differed significantly, t(99.8) = 3.12, p = .002, d = 0.62, 95% CI [0.23, 1.01]. Levene's test indicated equal variances (F = 0.34, p = .561); Shapiro-Wilk tests confirmed normality in both groups (both p > .05).

output_examples.input: What sample size do I need for my study with a medium effect size?
output_examples.output: For an independent-samples t-test targeting Cohen's d = 0.50 with alpha = 0.05 and power = 0.80, the required sample size is approximately 64 participants per group (128 total). For 90% power at the same effect size, this increases to approximately 86 participants per group (172 total). A sensitivity analysis shows that with 50 participants per group, your study can detect effect sizes of d = 0.57 or larger at 80% power.

output_examples.input: Explain the assumption checks for my ANOVA with three groups.
output_examples.output: For a one-way ANOVA with three groups, three key assumptions must be verified. First, normality: the Shapiro-Wilk test should be non-significant for each group (p > .05), supported by Q-Q plots. Second, homogeneity of variance: Levene's test should be non-significant (p > .05). Third, independence of observations, which is ensured by study design. If assumptions are violated, consider Welch's ANOVA, Kruskal-Wallis test, or data transformation as alternatives.

best_practices: Always check statistical assumptions (normality, homoscedasticity, independence) before interpreting test results. Report which checks were performed and any corrective actions taken.
Report effect sizes with confidence intervals alongside p-values. Effect sizes show practical significance while p-values only indicate statistical significance.
Perform power analysis during study planning, not after data collection. Post-hoc power analysis based on observed p-values is statistically invalid.
anti_patterns: Running multiple post-hoc comparisons without correcting for familywise error rate. Always use Bonferroni, Tukey HSD, or false discovery rate corrections when conducting multiple tests.
Using post-hoc power analysis computed from observed effect sizes and p-values. Instead, use sensitivity analysis to determine the effect size your study could detect.
Choosing a statistical test after examining the data to find significant results (p-hacking). Pre-register your analysis plan and select tests based on research design, not outcomes.

faq.question: What statistical tests does this skill cover?
faq.answer: The skill covers t-tests, ANOVA, chi-square, Fisher's exact test, correlation (Pearson and Spearman), linear and logistic regression, non-parametric alternatives (Mann-Whitney, Kruskal-Wallis, Wilcoxon), and Bayesian analysis methods.

faq.question: Does this skill run Python code automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides guidance, code templates, and reference documentation. The AI assistant writes Python code that you can run in your environment. The skill itself does not execute code.

faq.question: Can this skill analyze my dataset directly?
faq.answer: The skill cannot access external files. You need to describe your data structure and provide summary statistics or sample data within the conversation for the AI to guide your analysis.

faq.question: What is APA-style statistical reporting?
faq.answer: APA style requires reporting test statistics with degrees of freedom, exact p-values, effect sizes, and confidence intervals. The skill provides templates and examples to format results correctly for publication.

faq.question: Does this skill support Bayesian statistics?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance on Bayesian analysis with posterior distributions, credible intervals, Bayes Factors, prior selection, and convergence diagnostics. It references pymc and arviz libraries.

faq.question: What if my data violates normality assumptions?
faq.answer: The skill recommends non-parametric alternatives such as Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis, or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests. It also covers data transformations (log, Box-Cox), robust methods, and bootstrapping as remedies.
